revision.text: #redirect[[en:Wooden_Coffins]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
#REDIRECT [[棺]]

'''木棺'''（もっかん）とは、木でつくった[[棺]]をいう
== 概要 == 板材を組み合わせてつくった組合式（くみあわせしき）と木材を刳り抜いて蓋と身をつくった刳抜式（くりぬきしき）とがある。
組合式木棺は[[縄文時代晩期後半に稲作文化の構成要素の一つで、[[朝鮮半島]]から伝わり、現在まで用いられている。
刳抜式木棺は、割竹形（わりたけがた）木棺を中心に、[[弥生時代]]終わり頃に朝鮮半島から伝来し、古墳時代初め終わり終わりまで多用された。
両者とも遺体を仰向けや横向けに収める'''寝棺'''であるが、[[江戸時代]]以降には座った姿勢で納める'''座棺'''も出現した<ref>座った姿勢で埋葬することは弥生時代中・後期の吉備や北海道地方でもみられる。</ref>。 座棺には平面円形と方形のものがあり、円形のものは早棺や桶棺、方形のものは立棺や箱棺などと呼ばれる。
== 種類 == *組合式は箱形が一般的で、縄文時代晩期の西日本に出現し、弥生時代以降は西日本で発達した。
*刳抜式 **[[割竹形木棺]]小型のものが弥生時代後期に出現した。 **[[舟形木棺]]は刳り抜きしにに入り、底が丸形のものはと呼ばれる。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == *田中琢・佐原真編『日本考古学事典』三省堂 2003年 ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8 *永原慶二監修 石上英一他編集『岩波 日本史辞典』岩波書店 1999年 ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8
== 関連項目 == *[[棺]] *[[石棺墓]] *[[木棺墓]] *[[古墳]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:せつかん}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:縄文時代]] [[Category:弥生時代]] [[Category:古墳時代]]

'''木棺'''（もっかん）とは、木でつくった[[棺]]をいう
== 概要 == 板材を組み合わせてつくった組合式（くみあわせしき）と木材を刳り抜いて蓋と身をつくった刳抜式（くりぬきしき）とがある。
組合式木棺は[[縄文時代]]晩期後半に稲作文化の構成要素の一つで、[[朝鮮半島]]から伝わり、現在まで用いられている。
刳抜式木棺は、割竹形（わりたけがた）木棺を中心に、[[弥生時代]]終わり頃に朝鮮半島から伝来し、古墳時代初め終わり終わりまで多用された。
両者とも遺体を仰向けや横向けに収める'''寝棺'''であるが、[[江戸時代]]以降には座った姿勢で納める'''座棺'''も出現した<ref>座った姿勢で埋葬することは弥生時代中・後期の吉備や北海道地方でもみられる。</ref>。 座棺には平面円形と方形のものがあり、円形のものは早棺や桶棺、方形のものは立棺や箱棺などと呼ばれる。
== 種類 == *組合式は箱形が一般的で、縄文時代晩期の西日本に出現し、弥生時代以降は西日本で発達した。

'''木棺'''（もっかん）とは、遺骸を収める[[棺]]を木材で造ったもの。
== 概要 == 棺には、木棺の他に壺棺・甕棺・石棺・陶棺・漆棺などがある。</BR>材料の入手性や加工の容易さ、[[火葬]]の習慣などから、日本では古代から絶えることなく使用されている。
板材を組み合わせて造られた組合式（くみあわせしき）と木材を刳り抜いて蓋と身を造った刳抜式（くりぬきしき）とがある。</BR> 組合式木棺は、[[縄文時代]]晩期後半に[[稲作]]とともに北九州に渡来し、現在まで用いられている。
刳抜式木棺は、割竹形（わりたけがた）木棺を中心に、[[弥生時代]]終わり頃に大陸から伝来し、[[古墳時代]]初期では多用されたていたが、後期には[[箱形木棺]]に取って代わられた。
組合式木棺・刳抜式木棺は、とも遺体を仰向けや横向けに収める'''寝棺'''であるが、[[江戸時代]]以降には座った姿勢で納める'''座棺'''も出現した<ref>座った姿勢で埋葬することは弥生時代中・後期の吉備や北海道地方でも見られる。</ref>。</br> 座棺には平面円形と方形のものがあり、円形のものは早棺や桶棺、方形のものは立棺や箱棺などと呼ばれる。
== 種類 == *組合式は箱形が一般的で、縄文時代晩期の西日本に出現し、弥生時代以降は西日本で発達した。
*刳抜式 **[[割竹形木棺]]：小型のものが弥生時代後期に出現した。 **[[舟形木棺]] ：弥生時代や古墳時代に見られる。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == *田中琢・佐原真編『日本考古学事典』三省堂 2003年 ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8 *永原慶二監修 石上英一他編集『岩波 日本史辞典』岩波書店 1999年 ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8
== 関連項目 == *[[古墳]] *[[棺]] *[[石棺]] *[[石棺墓]] *[[木棺墓]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:せつかん}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:縄文時代]] [[Category:弥生時代]] [[Category:古墳時代]]

材料の入手性や加工の容易さ、[[火葬]]の習慣などから、日本では古代から絶えることなく使用されている。 板材を組み合わせて造られた組合式（くみあわせしき）と木材を刳り抜いて蓋と身を造った刳抜式（くりぬきしき）とがある。
組合式木棺は、[[縄文時代]]晩期後半に[[稲作]]とともに北九州に渡来し、現在まで用いられている。 刳抜式木棺は、割竹形（わりたけがた）木棺を中心に、[[弥生時代]]終わり頃に大陸から伝来し、[[古墳時代]]初期では多用されたていたが、後期には[[箱形木棺]]に取って代わられた。 組合式木棺・刳抜式木棺は、ともに遺体を仰向けや横向けに納める'''寝棺'''であるが、[[江戸時代]]以降には座った姿勢で納める'''座棺'''も出現した座った姿勢で埋葬することは弥生時代中・後期の吉備や北海道地方でも見られる。。
座棺には平面円形と方形のものがあり、円形のものは早棺や桶棺、方形のものは立棺や箱棺などと呼ばれる。 == 種類 == *組合式は箱形が一般的で、縄文時代晩期の西日本に出現し、弥生時代以降は西日本で発達した。 *刳抜式 **[[割竹形木棺]]：小型のものが弥生時代後期に出現した。 **[[舟形木棺]]　 ：弥生時代や古墳時代に見られる。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == *田中琢・佐原真編『日本考古学事典』三省堂　2003年　ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8 *永原慶二監修　石上英一他編集『岩波　日本史辞典』岩波書店　1999年　ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8 == 関連項目 == *[[古墳]] *[[棺]] *[[石棺]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|石棺墓]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|木棺墓]] {{DEFAULTSORT:もつかん}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:縄文時代]] [[Category:弥生時代]] [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:棺]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose {{ill|remains|ja|遺骸}}. == Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|jar coffins|ja|壺棺}}, {{ill|urn coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}. Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuou..."
revision.text:
'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose {{ill|remains|ja|遺骸}}.
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|jar coffins|ja|壺棺}}, {{ill|urn coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: **assembled wooden coffins** (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and **carved wooden coffins** (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are **reclining coffins** (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, **seated coffins** (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * **Split bamboo-shaped coffins**: Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * **Boat-shaped coffins**: Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Yayoi period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Wood materials]]
[[ja:木棺]]

Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are **reclining coffins** (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, **seated coffins** (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>

Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are **reclining coffins** (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, **seated coffins** (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * **Split bamboo-shaped coffins**: Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * **Boat-shaped coffins**: Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose {{ill|Cadaver|lt=remains|ja|遺骸}}.
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|jar coffins|ja|壺棺}}, {{ill|Jar burial|lt=urn coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|sarcophagus|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|Sarcophagus|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|jar coffins|ja|壺棺}}, {{ill|Jar burial|lt=urn coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''Split bamboo-shaped coffins''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''Boat-shaped coffins''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

revision.text:
'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|漆棺}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.

Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

revision.text:
'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Trimming off this bad category]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.

Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

revision.text:
'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>

== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

'''Wooden Coffin''' (木棺, Mokkan) refers to a coffin made from {{ill|wood|ja|木材}} used to enclose [[:en:remains|remains]].
== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|ja|棺}} * {{ill|sekkan|ja|石棺}} * {{ill|Stone coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden coffin tombs in Yayoi burial practices|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}}

Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=draft}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

== Overview == In addition to wooden coffins, there are {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, [[:en:Jar burial|urn coffins]], {{ill|sekkan|lt=stone coffins|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|ceramic coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|lacquer coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.
Due to the availability of wood, ease of processing, and the custom of cremation, wooden coffins have been continuously used in Japan since ancient times.
Wooden coffins are classified into two types: '''assembled wooden coffins''' (組合式木棺, Kumiawase-shiki Mokkan), made by joining planks, and '''carved wooden coffins''' (刳抜式木棺, Kurinuki-shiki Mokkan), made by carving a single piece of wood into a body and lid.
Assembled wooden coffins were introduced to northern Kyushu in the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} alongside rice cultivation and continue to be used today. Carved wooden coffins, including {{ill|split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=draft}}, were introduced from the continent in the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and were widely used in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, later replaced by box-shaped coffins during the later Kofun period.
Both assembled and carved wooden coffins are '''reclining coffins''' (寝棺, Nekan), used to lay the deceased flat or sideways. However, starting in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, '''seated coffins''' (座棺, Zakan) for burying the deceased in a sitting posture also appeared.<ref>Sitting burials were also observed in the mid-to-late Yayoi period in regions like Kibi and Hokkaido.</ref>
Seated coffins include circular and rectangular variants. Circular coffins are known as "early coffins" or "barrel coffins," while rectangular ones are referred to as "standing coffins" or "box coffins."
== Types == === Assembled Wooden Coffins === Typically box-shaped, these first appeared in western Japan during the late Jōmon period and developed further in the Yayoi period and beyond.
=== Carved Wooden Coffins === * '''[[Waritakegata Mokkan|Split bamboo-shaped coffins]]''': Small versions appeared in the late Yayoi period. * '''[[Funagata Sekkan|Boat-shaped coffins]]''': Seen during the Yayoi and Kofun periods.
== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.

== References == <references />
== Suggested Readings == * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds., *Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten* (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology), Sanseidō, 2003, ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Nagahara Keiji (supervising editor), Ishigami Eiichi et al., *Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten* (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary), Iwanami Shoten, 1999, ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8.
